front_text: Cervical insufficiency (also called incompetent cervix) is a condition in which the cervix is too weak to remain closed during pregnancy.
front_html: Cervical insufficiency (also called {{c1::incompetent cervix}}) is a condition in which the cervix is too weak to remain closed during pregnancy.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Cervical insufficiency
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Cervical insufficiency

front_text: In cervical insufficiency, the cervix undergoes painless dilation or shortening, typically during the second trimester, without painful contractions.
front_html: In cervical insufficiency, the cervix undergoes {{c1::painless}} dilation or shortening, typically during the {{c1::second}} trimester, without painful contractions.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Cervical insufficiency
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Cervical insufficiency

front_text: In cervical insufficiency, premature cervical opening leads to painless dilation, membrane rupture, and miscarriage or preterm birth because the uterus can no longer sustain the pregnancy.
front_html: In cervical insufficiency, premature cervical opening leads to {{c1::painless}} dilation, {{c1::membrane}} rupture, and {{c1::miscarriage}} or {{c1::preterm birth}} because the uterus can no longer sustain the pregnancy.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Cervical insufficiency
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Cervical insufficiency

front_text: Cervical insufficiency most often presents as recurrent second-trimester pregnancy losses occurring at approximately 16–24 weeks gestation with little or no pain beforehand.
front_html: Cervical insufficiency most often presents as recurrent {{c1::second-trimester}} pregnancy losses occurring at approximately {{c1::16–24}} weeks gestation with little or no pain beforehand.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Cervical insufficiency
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Cervical insufficiency

front_text: On physical exam, cervical insufficiency may show painless dilation of the cervix, often with minimal bleeding or discharge.
front_html: On physical exam, cervical insufficiency may show {{c1::painless dilation}} of the cervix, often with minimal bleeding or discharge.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Cervical insufficiency
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Cervical insufficiency

front_text: Risk factor for cervical insufficiency: prior cervical trauma or surgery, including cone biopsy, LEEP, or repeated dilation and curettage (D&C).
front_html: Risk factor for cervical insufficiency: prior cervical trauma or surgery, including {{c1::cone biopsy}}, {{c1::LEEP}}, or repeated {{c1::dilation and curettage (D&C)}}.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Cervical insufficiency
extra:
Other risk factors:
• congenital cervical abnormalities
• diethylstilbestrol (DES) exposure
• uterine anomalies
• overdistension (e.g., multiple gestation or polyhydramnios)
• history of cervical insufficiency in a prior pregnancy
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Cervical insufficiency

front_text: Risk factor for cervical insufficiency: congenital cervical abnormalities.

front_text: The preferred diagnostic modality for cervical insufficiency is transvaginal ultrasound.
front_html: The preferred diagnostic modality for cervical insufficiency is {{c1::transvaginal ultrasound}}.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Cervical insufficiency
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Cervical insufficiency

front_text: Transvaginal ultrasound for cervical insufficiency is typically performed between 16–24 weeks gestation, often as part of the 18–22 week anatomy scan.
front_html: Transvaginal ultrasound for cervical insufficiency is typically performed between {{c1::16–24}} weeks gestation, often as part of the {{c1::18–22}} week anatomy scan.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Cervical insufficiency
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Cervical insufficiency

front_text: On transvaginal ultrasound, a normal cervical length is ≥ 30 mm, while a length of  mm before 24 weeks is considered a concerning finding for cervical insufficiency.
front_html: On transvaginal ultrasound, a normal cervical length is {{c1::≥ 30}} mm, while a length of {{c1::< 25}} mm before 24 weeks is considered a concerning finding for cervical insufficiency.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Cervical insufficiency
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Cervical insufficiency

front_text: On transvaginal ultrasound, cervical insufficiency may show funneling, in which the internal os begins to open and appears as a V or U shape while the external os remains closed.
front_html: On transvaginal ultrasound, cervical insufficiency may show {{c1::funneling}}, in which the internal os begins to open and appears as a {{c1::V}} or {{c1::U}} shape while the external os remains closed.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Cervical insufficiency
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Cervical insufficiency

front_text: The primary treatment for cervical insufficiency is cervical cerclage, a surgical stitch placed around the cervix to keep it closed.
front_html: The primary treatment for cervical insufficiency is {{c1::cervical cerclage}}, a surgical stitch placed around the cervix to keep it closed.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Cervical insufficiency
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Cervical insufficiency

front_text: In cervical insufficiency, cervical cerclage is typically placed at 12–16 weeks of gestation and removed at approximately 36–38 weeks to allow for normal labor.
front_html: In cervical insufficiency, cervical cerclage is typically placed at {{c1::12–16}} weeks of gestation and removed at approximately {{c1::36–38}} weeks to allow for normal labor.
